title: Keisner Eyeing Audi Exit? Japanese GP Winner Reportedly Considering Options
meta: Published: 5 April 2026 | By Olly Bradshaw, Paddock Insider | F1CC News
caption: Robin Keisner celebrates his maiden victory in Japan — but could it be his first and last with Audi?
body:
Robin Keisner is looking for a way out of Audi.

That is the rumour that has begun circulating in the days following his extraordinary victory at Suzuka, and it has not been silenced by the team's public denials.

Audi's higher‑ups have dismissed the speculation as “unfounded background noise.”

Background noise. That's what they're calling it.

But sources close to the situation suggest the 22‑year‑old's representatives have been quietly exploring the landscape. Three potential destinations have emerged: Ferrari, Red Bull, and Mercedes.

Three of the four biggest teams on the grid. All potentially in the market for a driver of Keisner's profile.

A Career at the Crossroads

Keisner's journey to this point has been anything but linear. 19th in the championship with Alpine in 2024, scoring 26 points in a car that offered little room to shine. Then the move to Sauber — now Audi — for 2025, where he collected 67 points and finished 17th. It was a step up, but a modest one. The promise of the Audi project, with its full manufacturer backing and long‑term ambition, was meant to be the turning point.

And in many ways, it has been. Four rounds into 2026, Keisner sits second in the championship with 41 points. He has won a grand prix — Japan, in the rain, a drive of flawless composure. He has out‑scored his teammate Richard Hicks by 32 points. He has been Driver of the Day twice.

Yet the questions persist.

Audi's difficulties in sustaining momentum — the early qualifying struggles, the operational missteps, the sense that every result is hard‑won rather than inevitable — have left Keisner, according to those who know him, weighing whether the project can deliver on its promises quickly enough.

He is 22. He is in the form of his life. And his career is at a crossroads.

Stay with Audi, the team that gave him his breakthrough, and hope the infrastructure catches up to his talent? Or move now, while his stock is at its highest, to a team that can offer a championship‑ready car?

The Suitors

Ferrari are the most intriguing destination. The Scuderia have one seat locked down — Eetu Väisänen, the championship leader — and another that has become the most scrutinised position on the grid. Afonso Cachinho has not scored a point. He has six penalty points. His seat is under open review. Keisner, in a Ferrari, would give the team a proven points‑scorer alongside their championship leader. The pairing of Väisänen and Keisner would be, on current form, the strongest on the grid.

Red Bull's interest is less obvious but no less real. Jason Roy has shown flashes of brilliance — pole in China, pace in qualifying — but has not scored since his podium in Shanghai. Philippe Verhaeren has two points finishes in four races. The team sits seventh in the constructors' championship. A driver of Keisner's consistency would be a stabilising force. And Red Bull have never been shy about making driver changes when the numbers demand it.

Mercedes, meanwhile, are in a state of internal turbulence. Maikel Zwart's “shopping trolley” comment and his collision with Joshua Nathanial in Japan have strained relationships. Wesley Vandesteene has been the team's brighter light, with 33 points and a podium at the last race. But the Brackley squad are eighth in the constructors', and their engine upgrade has not yet delivered the step forward they promised. Keisner to Mercedes would be a statement of intent — a young, marketable, race‑winning driver joining a team desperate to rebuild.

Audi's Position

Audi's public stance is firm. The speculation is unfounded. Keisner is under contract. The project is on track.

Privately, paddock observers note that Audi have invested heavily in Keisner — not just financially, but in building the team around his feedback. Losing him now, just as the results are starting to come, would be a devastating blow. The team's constructors' position — second, with 50 points — is built largely on his shoulders.

But contracts in Formula 1 are not unbreakable. Performance clauses exist. Buyout options exist. If Keisner wants to leave, and if one of the big three is willing to pay, the path is there.

No formal talks are believed to have taken place. Yet.

The key word is “yet.”

The Decision

Keisner has not commented. His management have not commented. The teams named — Ferrari, Red Bull, Mercedes — have all declined to respond.

The silence is not unusual. Driver market negotiations are rarely conducted in public. But the timing is significant. Keisner's victory in Japan has made him the hottest property on the grid not named Väisänen. His value will never be higher. The decision he faces — loyalty to the project that backed him, or ambition for a car that can win now — is the defining choice of his career.

He left Alpine for Sauber because he saw a brighter future. He was right.

Now the question is whether he sees a brighter future still, somewhere else.

Watch this space.
